speaker: interviewer
text: Tape four, August 7, 1996, Michael Lipenholtz. You were telling us about how you found out what had happened to your parents.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was going on a-- on a mission or something anyway, and-- and it came a guy from Vilna. He recognized me right away. I said, what's going on over there? He says, they're liquidating the ghetto. Do you know about-- about someplace where the bunker was, where we had the parents in the bunker? He says, they're breaking the bunker. They find somebody in the-- in the-- in this-- in the-- in the vicinity. And the guy says, let me go. And I'll show you where they've got a lot of people. And they start breaking us. So we stopped the thing, the-- what we had to do. And we decided to go through the other-- the people on the base from the-- from the party, they wouldn't let us go. Because we know too much. Because if they catch you, they will-- they'll torture you. And you will tell them where we are. [? Big ?] [? deal, ?] anyway. We say, OK. You wouldn't let us in. So, we'll-- we'll come to someplace else. So we went. And we came to-- to-- to Vilna. And there-- there was a place where they used to make fur things for the-- for the soldiers, you know, on the front. And [SIGHS] it was a place where the Jews were still working in the ghetto, otherwise it was liquidated. But these people were still working. And we got in over there. And they said, they're breaking the bunker. They couldn't break it in. Because it was something we did. It was like a cement thing, it used to come in. We came from the other side. When we came in, we find out that my friend's, [? Greens ?] mother, had a place, someplace in the-- in the country. But they didn't tell me about it. And I didn't ask him, what-- you know, is there such a bunker, then we'll come. Anyway, we came into-- to [? Kylis, ?] where we used to make the fur, used for the soldiers. And we were there, and I find out from this girl. Her-- her-- her father was a [NON-ENGLISH] in Vilna, a big deal, you know. And he was there with-- with my parents, together with maybe a hundred people in the bunker. This was a bunker between-- between the ghetto and the outside. You should be able to get in through the-- through the sewers, you know, sewers over there. You had to go and walk over there in the sewers. But you couldn't walk straight. You know, you had to-- And my father and mother was there, and his mother went away with the other people from-- from a town, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he didn't have to go. So I had to go myself. So I find the-- the girl. She was the-- the [NON-ENGLISH] daughter. And she knew them. She was a brave girl. And she knew everything. But she-- but she belonged to the organization. And then she had some--

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyway, we went to go over there. She didn't have to do it. Because she had papers, you know, a girl could have papers. If she had papers, good papers, she didn't have to. But a guy couldn't, you know. But [INAUDIBLE] you're Jewish or not. So I went with her in the streets. We got into the bunker, you know. And we came-- came into the bunker, you know. Nobody was there. A woman that got left over with a baby, she couldn't-- she didn't have no place to go. And my father was religious, I told you. So when he finds in the bunker this, he offered her [NON-ENGLISH], you know. So they went out together, and they went to another-- some other bunker. And over there they got catched. For everything I done, nothing. So I was left like this. Then I took her-- and [? Kylis ?] was two places, you know, two buildings. So, I was-- used the girl, you know. So I took her over there. And I forgot where I am. And then I went back. In the meantime, it got dark. And after darkness, you couldn't go out in the streets. This was yet in-- in the Jewish camps, and they had a lot of [NON-ENGLISH] you call it. You know, they catch us, who catch the Jews, and you know, and took them to Ponary, or you had to buy out yourself, if you had money, a lot of money.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I went and-- and took her there. And I back, you know, to-- to the place where I was sleeping over there. And on the way over there, you see leaving-- so, you know, in the streets over there. So I'm going on this side. And then what did they have on the other side? Two big policeman.

speaker: interviewee
text: They used to call them [NON-ENGLISH], [NON-ENGLISH], you know, it's a [NON-ENGLISH]. They were supposed to tall and big. And they-- I'm going on this side, and this I remember. And they are going on this side. And on this side where they are going, is the-- is the [? Kylis. ?] The [? Kylis, ?] you know, the-- the place where the people who were-- were still working. They used to make the fur.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I see what's going on. They are two, and I am one, and nobody else on the streets. So I figure, that's it, you know. They're not going to wait for me, they'll see with a guy. So I figure, it's no good. But what do I got to lose? I took my gun out, I-- I put them in the pocket. I had a-- I had a jacket, you know, like we were in the city. This we didn't wear in the woods. And I figure, [NON-ENGLISH]. But I know what I'm going to do. And they don't know yet what-- what to expect. And I went on the other side, and they smacked a [LAUGHS] [INAUDIBLE] right between the [NON-ENGLISH]. Cops smell that good? And they say to me, [NON-ENGLISH], I figure, that's it. Nobody there. And you know what? The worst thing is when you've got one by yourself. You know, when you have to go like, die or something. When you're by yourself it's a very feeling, you got tense, you know. And this is-- [? you see it. ?] You know, they're professionals. But they went and then, and they light me in the eyes. And they say, [NON-ENGLISH]. [NON-ENGLISH] means, excuse me?

speaker: interviewee
text: Maybe they had a feeling. I was ready. And I went over, and this was a fence. It was a 6 foot, maybe 6 and 1/2. And I was so excited, I jumped it over. You know, like-- like. That was-- you had a feeling. You get-- you get excited. But by yourself, it's way bad, you know, to take a chance like this. Or you're going out, and dead, you know. That's what happened. I jumped over the fence again. And then-- then-- I could stay over there. They asked me to say, you know. I should stay, you know, live over there. They had-- a normal life almost, you know, in this Kylis, where they make the furs. I couldn't stay there. I was there a week. The next week I couldn't stay anymore. I say, I'm going back. So he says, he's going back too, this guy, you know, [NON-ENGLISH]. And we went back. We couldn't stay. We-- everything was not like-- it wasn't like in the woods, you know. Couldn't stay. It was very-- I couldn't take it anymore.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened to your brother?

speaker: interviewee
text: My brother was a different story. My brother had a wife and a kid. And him had-- had protection from-- from his father-in-law. And he got a job like a chimney cleaner. This was a special job, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they took the Jews, and they didn't get paid. And they used to go and clean the chimneys. So this was a big deal, you know. They could go out, you know, and buy him something what they needed, you know. A chimney cleaner, they went by themselves. What you are going to go, in a group? So he had a job. He was lucky.

speaker: interviewee
text: And when it came to liquidation, they took the whole group, the chimney cleaners, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and then this man, this friend of mine, the comisar, you know, [NON-ENGLISH], he could do something. But he didn't. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And you know what was the end of him? He was a comisar, a big comisar. And the-- they shot him, I think. He died over there, in the-- in the-- oh, this was politics, already, you know. This was politics. This was the Communist Party. And the other parties, and they were looking to get rid of him, and they said he was working with the Germans. It wasn't true. Because he was with me at this time.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they-- they killed a whole bunch of people, the Jews killed the Jews. This-- this was a comisar. He had the guys like-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] from the police something. And they used to fight between themselves, you know, in the ghettos yet. And then some they got from the top, it came from the top of that-- that they should kill him. And they killed him. I didn't want to tell you about this.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened to you after the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: After the war I was all right. I was all right.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you go?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was in Vilna first of all.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then?

speaker: interviewee
text: And then I make a couple dollars. And then, we left for to-- to go to Israel. This was the only way to think about it. And-- and they made me for a big shot over there, in Bari.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where is that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Bari was [NON-ENGLISH] south of Italy. They had a kibbutz over there. I was the menahel.

speaker: interviewer
text: That was in Italy?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Italy, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was-- what did you do as the menahel?

speaker: interviewee
text: I headed the kibbutz, I headed-- this. I was living in Italy eight years. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I had a good life over there. I used to make a good living. And then I got married. And she came from Israel to-- to me. I had accident. A blow out.

speaker: interviewer
text: You told me you had seen somebody executed in Italy.

speaker: interviewee
text: In Italy? Not in Italy, in the woods. Oh, here? Oh! This was Mussolini. But they didn't hang him by the throat, they hang him from the feet.

speaker: interviewer
text: They hung him upside down?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you know it was Mussolini?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't know. Everybody was talking about it. This is a big city, you know, Milan. And they were talking about his hanging. The partisans in-- in the Alps, catched him. He was escaping to-- to Germany. And they catched him. And they brought him over there, and they hanged him by the feet.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now you just said that you had seen people hung or executed before. In the woods?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, not in the woods.

speaker: interviewer
text: It's that where you had seen it?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, not in the woods.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think I saw it, when they're they hang the people in-- in Vilna in a place, in the-- not in [? Kylis, ?] [? HKP. ?] It was where they fixed-- used to fix the cars. They used to hang people over there, Jewish, they used to hang, I think.

speaker: interviewer
text: You saw Jews being hung?

speaker: interviewee
text: I knew it. But I didn't live there. You know, I went in, maybe [INAUDIBLE], maybe they shoot at me? [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] You got--

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. You were in Italy for eight years?

speaker: interviewee
text: Eight years.

speaker: interviewer
text: And?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't-- I didn't want to go to America. You know, I didn't want to. Because I had a good business, and I got married. But I had a-- a letter from my uncle. I wear [NON-ENGLISH] to me, he looked, you know what [NON-ENGLISH]? [LAUGHS] He-- he used to-- used to say, I had letters from from my aunt. They sent me papers, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was that before you were married or after?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- maybe at the same time, anyway.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what year was it when you got married?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] Don't ask me. I don't remember. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. So your uncle sent you papers. He wanted you to come to the United States.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Had you ever thought of going to Palestine?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was have-- was-- when I-- when I was the leader of the kibbutz, I was thinking of to go to Palestine, you know. This was the-- my mission. We had a big kibbutz. I was the leader for over there. And my-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] then they liked it. And all of a sudden, I had a kibbutz, you know. Everything was all right. So these-- these Israelis, you know, the Bricha, send over a guy, he should take it over. And he start giving orders, you know. And I wasn't used to it. Nobody gave me orders before.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I didn't want to do it. Anyway, he made his groups, you know, from-- from [INAUDIBLE] and [INAUDIBLE], and-- and [INAUDIBLE]. And he started doing things. And all of a sudden, they wanted to make election. It was no election before. So I figured, OK, make election. I was-- I'm not getting paid here. So they made election. And he won. I say, OK. Here, [NON-ENGLISH]. Keep it. So I forget, whatever I had to do. And I went on the-- on the stock market in Bari, in the city, start talking around. And some Jews from all over the world, escaped over there. And they remained-- remained alive, and they were-- doing business on the-- I figured, what can they do? You had the Jews. I see you in Milan.

speaker: interviewee
text: I figure I have a look at what's going on. I came there. I see a couple guys from-- from-- from way back, from the house. All of a sudden everybody lives normally. And they had a good life.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I decided here I'm out of a job. They don't pay me, but I'm out. And I figure I over there and have a look. I came, and they live normal already. I wasted my time here, a year, [LAUGHS] for nothing, you know, for-- for the government.

speaker: interviewee
text: I went over there. And I'll tell another story. You have to-- you see, they come-- the people came over there. Some of them make some money, you know, on the way. They make-- make some business, you know. And some didn't. But some started, you know, selling cigarettes and this. And they were good at it. I see I had-- I was almost out of money. And I start-- I wanted to-- I wasn't good. I couldn't make it. You know, I couldn't make it a penny in business.

speaker: interviewer
text: So all of a sudden-- I couldn't do nothing. So everybody who couldn't do nothing was in the camps. Over there, you could sleep, you could eat, you know. And could do something. Anyway, I couldn't.

speaker: interviewee
text: I met some people from Bari, you know, some Jews. And they-- in Bari was on the other side of the water, you know, we are the Poles. And the Poles have family, you know, and the Poles from the-- from the Shin Bet.

speaker: interviewer
text: You know what Shin Bet is? The Poles were from Shin Bet. They had a lot of money, Shin Bet, you know. That's the Italian-- the secret police. And they had a lot of money. And they had families in Poland. And nobody went to Poland at this time.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because you didn't have no papers. You didn't have no government. You didn't have it in Italy. So nobody went. But they had families. The Jewish had families. And the Poles had families. And nobody would go. You know, because the [NON-ENGLISH], the Russian were there. And they-- and I figured out how much I can take from this side, and from this side. I figure, I haven't got nothing else to do. So I went without papers, without nothing. I didn't have no papers at all. And-- and even though I'm Jewish, nothing. I throw everything out. And I went to Poland. And I came over there.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: What year was this? I got it written someplace anyway. And I came, and I was 16 days in Poland. In this 16 days, there was pogroms on Jews. They used to stop the trains, take out the Jews, shoot them, on the trains. This was-- and Kielce was the pogrom. And in any place-- and they told me-- listen, you're going. Don't go. But I figure, I have to-- have to do it. And I went. And in 16 days I brought everybody together, and I brought everybody to Italy. And the way they gave me a kibbutz, I should take with me, you know. They should help me. So they-- I should take a kibbutz. They find the [NON-ENGLISH], you know, [NON-ENGLISH]. That's what I-- so I took him. I took him with me. And I brought him to Poland in 16 days. And I made a bundle. And everybody paid. You see, the-- I wouldn't take money from them in advance. But maybe they wouldn't give it to me. But they passed the money, you know, some people. And this was already a different story, you know. You got money, and everything is good. Then I had an accident. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They thought I was dead. And it was in the papers, in the Italian papers. And I came back alive. And I'm here.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your fiancee?

speaker: interviewee
text: My fiancee came-- when-- when it was in the papers, she couldn't get out of Israel then. It was no such a thing like out. But when she had the paper, my friend sent her a paper, you know, Italian paper, that certain this one and that one is [NON-ENGLISH]. It means he's going. And I didn't feel like I'm going. [LAUGHS] And I came back. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I had my head open from here to here. I had my face knocked in. I had my leg broken. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And the rest of it, ah-- but I wasn't scared.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why not?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. I was in the hospital. I had money. You know, over there you have money, they give you the best service. Then I was laying in bed over there one night, and I figure, and I saw the-- you know, the-- the [NON-ENGLISH]. What do you call it? You know, the picture of how it looks. But I couldn't tell to a guy, a doctor that-- that he made it wrong. So I was-- was waiting at night to a resident, you know, a young doctor. I say, let's make a bet.

speaker: interviewer
text: I figured everything he would send out, so I make a bet. And you know what it is? It looked like this. This is the bone, let's say, this one, like this. So when you look this way, it looked like this, good, you you, from when I show you. But when you take another picture this way, it was like this. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: And they say, they cannot do nothing. This was Udine, a city, near the frontier. But when you got the money, I say I'll pay. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So they took me from Udine they took me to Bologna. Bologna is the biggest orthopedic hospital in-- in Europe. And over there, they took me in. They put-- put weights on you, and they put me in the bath. They just pulled.

speaker: interviewee
text: And when it's pulled out, you see when you break a big bone, the muscles are stretching it, pulling. So they had to stretch it out like this, till it come like this. And then they put it in a cast. And this was three months I was laying over there with a cast. But I didn't care. I had a good appetite. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you tell us how you met your fiancee?

speaker: interviewee
text: I met her before.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right, where?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Vilna, sure.

speaker: interviewer
text: And after you got married, how long did you stay in Italy?

speaker: interviewee
text: Did we stay a couple of years? No, maybe a year or something. I had the papers. You didn't-- then you got a wife, you cannot stay over there like-- [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the name of the aunt and uncle who sent you the papers from the United States?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, [INAUDIBLE]. I forgot.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. You're going to show us a picture of them later, and you'll tell us more about them then. And when you came to the United States, where did you live?

speaker: interviewee
text: Where everybody lived, we went to New York.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did you do for a living?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I had a contracting, used to make something. I made good, you know. And everything was like this. But I had-- still my leg was-- not so good. If you got a-- I'm telling you. You should know it. If you got something with the knee, that-- that never heals 100%, you know. You see, a knee is the-- is the worst part in your body. And that's what I had.

speaker: interviewer
text: You see, if you put a-- a knee in a cast, more than a couple of months, it never heals like it was. Because it's not a finished part. You see what I mean? That's what I'm-- I'm telling you. You should know it for somebody, not for yourself. OK. And tell us how many children you have.

speaker: interviewee
text: What do you mean? I got two.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. What are their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: Shelley-- Shelley and Charles, this I know. Oh, boy.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. We'd like to see pictures of your family.

speaker: interviewee
text: Huh?

speaker: interviewer
text: We'd like to see pictures now of your family from before the war, and then from after the war.

speaker: interviewee
text: She--

speaker: interviewer
text: We're going to-- thank you very much for the interview.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you. But she didn't-- she didn't show me?
